channel.title: Tutorial on Tobias Theel | Senior Software Engineer
channel.description: Recent content in Tutorial on Tobias Theel | Senior Software Engineer

item.title: Mastering Multi-File Tasks: A Guide to Gemini Code Assist Agent Mode
item.description:
Introduction

Software development often involves tasks that aren’t contained within a single function or file. Whether you’re refactoring a service layer, implementing a new API endpoint that requires changes from the database to the router, or updating dependencies across a monorepo, context is everything.

Standard AI chat assistants are great for snippets, but Gemini Code Assist’s Agent Mode is designed for the big picture. It doesn’t just suggest code; it plans and executes changes across your entire workspace.

item.title: The Architecture of Instructions: Mastering GEMINI.md
item.description:
Introduction

In my previous post, we explored how Gemini Code Assist’s Agent Mode can handle multi-file refactors. However, an agent is only as good as the instructions it follows. Without guidance, AI might generate code that works but violates your team’s architectural standards.

To solve this, Gemini supports a special file: GEMINI.md. Think of this as the “System Prompt” for your entire repository.

What is GEMINI.md?

The GEMINI.md file is a Markdown document placed in your project’s root. It serves as a persistent set of rules and context that the Agent reads before every task. It allows you to define:

item.title: How to check if a binary is statically or dynamically linked
item.description:
What is the difference between statically and dynamically linked binaries?

Statically linked binaries and dynamically linked binaries refer to two different ways of linking software libraries to an executable program.

A statically linked binary includes all necessary libraries within the binary executable file itself. This means that when the program is executed, it does not rely on any external libraries, as all required libraries are already included in the binary. Static linking produces larger binary files, but they can be more portable and self-contained.

item.title: Introduction to Hugo #1
item.description:
What is Hugo?

Hugo is a static site generator that offers several benefits for building fast and secure websites.

It has a fast build time, making it ideal for generating large sites quickly. Its intuitive interface and straightforward directory structure make it easy for users to get started with Hugo.

Additionally, Hugo provides flexibility through templates, shortcodes, and custom output formats, and can be easily deployed to a variety of hosting platforms.
